title: PDF attachments are corrupted after upload
body: When customers upload PDF files to their tasks, the files come through broken. You can't open them — the PDF viewer says the file is damaged. This is happening for multiple customers. We use the file attachment feature heavily so this is really impacting our workflow.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
Thanks for reporting this — corrupted attachments definitely sound disruptive, especially if your team relies on them heavily.

Could you walk me through what the process looks like when this happens? Specifically, what were you doing in TaskFlow when you uploaded the PDF — were you creating a new task, editing an existing one, dragging and dropping the file, using a file picker, etc.? And when you say the file comes through broken, are you seeing the corruption when you download it back from TaskFlow, or when another user tries to view it, or somewhere else?

Any detail about the flow from your side would be really helpful.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: Look, it doesn't matter how we upload them — drag and drop, file picker, whatever. The result is the same. The PDF is broken when anyone tries to download it again. We've had multiple customers complain this week. Can you just look into the upload system? Something is clearly wrong on your end.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
I hear you — we'll absolutely look into the upload system. The fact that multiple customers hit this the same week is actually a really useful clue.

Can you help me pin down the timing? You mentioned this started coming up this week — was PDF upload working fine before that? If so, do you have a rough sense of when the last successful upload was versus the first broken one? I'm trying to figure out whether something changed on our side that triggered this.

Also, is it every single PDF that comes back corrupted, or are some getting through fine? For instance, have you noticed any difference with smaller versus larger files?

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: Yeah it started about a week ago, things were fine before that. And now that you mention it, I think some smaller PDFs might be going through okay? But most of the ones our customers send are big contract documents, reports, that kind of thing, and those are all broken. I don't have exact dates for you. Can someone just check what changed in the upload system this past week?

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
That's really helpful — the timing and the size pattern together are strong clues. One more thing that would help us zero in on the right part of the upload system:

When you open one of these broken PDFs (or try to), what does the failure actually look like? For example, does the file appear to be cut short or smaller than the original? Does it look like it downloaded something but the content is garbled or unreadable? Or does it download as an empty or nearly empty file? If you happen to have the original file still, comparing the file sizes (the original versus what you get back from TaskFlow) would be really telling.

No pressure to do a deep investigation — even a rough sense of what you're seeing when you try to open one would help us point the developer at the right thing.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: The file size looks about the same as the original, maybe slightly different. It's not empty or cut short. The content is just... garbage. Like if you open it in a text editor it's all mangled characters. The PDF viewer just says it's damaged or corrupted. I don't have time to do file size comparisons for you — we have customers waiting on these documents. Can your dev team just look at whatever changed in the upload pipeline last week? That's obviously when this broke.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body: [RESOLVED] {"title":"PDF attachments corrupted on upload — binary encoding corruption affecting large files (regression ~1 week ago)","problem":"PDF files uploaded to tasks via any method (drag-and-drop, file picker) are returned corrupted when downloaded. The PDF viewer reports the file as damaged. The issue affects multiple customers and has been occurring for approximately one week. Larger files (contracts, reports) are consistently affected; smaller PDFs may still upload correctly.","root_cause_hypothesis":"A change to the upload/download pipeline approximately one week ago is applying a text encoding transformation (e.g., UTF-8 re-encoding, charset conversion, or a new middleware layer) to binary file data. The evidence strongly supports this: file sizes are roughly preserved (ruling out truncation or chunking issues) but content is garbled (ruling out partial upload or empty files). This is the classic signature of binary data being processed as text, where multi-byte sequences get reinterpreted or replaced. The size-dependent pattern suggests smaller files may survive the transformation by luck (fewer problematic byte sequences) or that a chunked upload threshold triggers different code paths.","reproduction_steps":["Upload a large PDF file (e.g., a multi-page contract or report, likely >1MB) to any task using either drag-and-drop or the file picker","Download the same file from the task","Attempt to open the downloaded PDF — it will be reported as damaged/corrupted","Open the downloaded file in a text editor and observe garbled/mangled characters","Compare with a small PDF upload (<100KB) which may succeed"],"environment":"Affects all customers, all upload methods, all PDF viewers. Not browser- or OS-specific based on the report of multiple customers being affected. Production environment.","severity":"high","impact":"Multiple customers are unable to share PDF documents (contracts, reports) through TaskFlow, which is a core workflow for their teams. Customers are waiting on these documents, making this a blocking issue for daily operations.","recommended_fix":"1. Review all changes to the file upload/download pipeline from the past 7-10 days (git log on upload service, storage layer, any API gateway or middleware changes). 2. Specifically look for: changes to content-type handling, new middleware that processes request/response bodies, encoding changes, streaming/buffering changes, or library upgrades that may have changed default encoding behavior. 3. Verify that binary files are being streamed as raw bytes (application/octet-stream) without any text encoding transformation at every stage: upload endpoint → storage → retrieval endpoint. 4. Check if a chunked upload threshold exists that routes large files through a different code path than small files. 5. Compare a hex dump of an original PDF with the corrupted download to confirm the encoding corruption pattern.","proposed_test_case":"Upload PDFs of varying sizes (100KB, 1MB, 5MB, 20MB) and verify byte-for-byte integrity by comparing SHA-256 checksums of the original and downloaded files. The test should cover both the upload and download paths and assert that the checksums match exactly.","information_gaps":["Exact file size threshold where corruption begins (reporter indicated 'smaller might be okay' but did not test systematically)","Whether non-PDF binary files (images, Word docs, ZIPs) are also affected","Exact date of first corrupted upload vs. last successful one (reporter couldn't provide precise dates)","Whether the corruption occurs at upload time (stored corrupted) or download time (served corrupted) — a developer can check by inspecting the stored blob directly"]}
